stem: Evaluate \( \int_{-[[h]]}^{[[h]]} \left( ([[c3]])x^{3} + ([[c2]])x^{2} + ([[c1]])x + ([[c0]]) \right) dx \), using the symmetry of the integrand over the symmetric interval.
solution: The odd-power terms \( ([[c3]])x^{3} \) and \( ([[c1]])x \) integrate to 0 over the symmetric interval \( [-[[h]], [[h]]] \). Only the even part contributes: \( \int_{-[[h]]}^{[[h]]} \left( ([[c2]])x^{2} + ([[c0]]) \right) dx = 2\left( \frac{[[c2]]}{3}[[h]]^{3} + ([[c0]])[[h]] \right) = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This integrates the even part only over \( [0, [[h]]] \), forgetting to double it.
This doubles the entire antiderivative, wrongly keeping the odd terms that actually cancel.
This keeps the doubled \( x^{2} \) term but drops the constant term's contribution.
This keeps only the constant term and drops the \( x^{2} \) contribution.
This is off by the full interval width.
This is off by one from the correct value.
This is off by one from the correct value.

stem: Without computing an antiderivative, use symmetry to evaluate \( \int_{-[[h]]}^{[[h]]} \left( ([[a]])x\cos([[b]]x) + ([[c]])x^{3} \right) dx \). (Note that \( x\cos([[b]]x) \) and \( x^{3} \) are both odd functions.)
solution: Both \( x\cos([[b]]x) \) (odd times even is odd) and \( x^{3} \) (odd) are odd functions, and a sum of odd functions is odd. The integral of any odd function over a symmetric interval \( [-[[h]], [[h]]] \) is exactly 0 — no antidifferentiation needed. So the integral equals \( [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This tries to double the cubic term's contribution as if it were even, instead of recognizing it vanishes.
This tries to double the cosine term's contribution as if it were even, instead of recognizing it vanishes.
This adds the coefficients as if the interval width mattered, ignoring the symmetry shortcut entirely.
This reports the half-width instead of recognizing the odd-function shortcut gives 0.
This guesses a generic nonzero constant instead of applying the odd-function shortcut.
This guesses a generic nonzero constant instead of applying the odd-function shortcut.
This multiplies the coefficients instead of recognizing the odd-function shortcut gives 0.

stem: Use the symmetry of sine (odd) and cosine (even) to evaluate \( \int_{-[[k]]\pi/2}^{[[k]]\pi/2} \left( ([[p]])\sin x + ([[q]])\cos x \right) dx \).
solution: \( \sin x \) is odd, so \( \int_{-[[k]]\pi/2}^{[[k]]\pi/2} ([[p]])\sin x\, dx = 0 \). \( \cos x \) is even, so its contribution doubles the half-integral: \( \int_{-[[k]]\pi/2}^{[[k]]\pi/2} ([[q]])\cos x\, dx = 2([[q]])\sin([[k]]\pi/2) \). The total is \( [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This wrongly doubles the SINE term's contribution, treating it as even instead of odd.
This keeps the sine term without doubling and drops the cosine term entirely.
This mixes up the coefficients, applying \( q \) where \( p \) belongs.
This uses \( \cos([[k]]\pi/2) \) instead of the correct \( \sin([[k]]\pi/2) \) from antidifferentiating cosine.
This is off by one from the correct value.
This is off by one from the correct value.
This is off by two from the correct value.

stem: For which value of \( k \) does \( \int_{-[[h]]}^{[[h]]} \left( ([[c3]])x^{3} + ([[c2]])x^{2} + ([[c1]])x + k \right) dx = 0 \)?
solution: By symmetry, the odd terms \( ([[c3]])x^{3} \) and \( ([[c1]])x \) always integrate to 0 over \( [-[[h]],[[h]]] \), regardless of \( k \). So the whole integral vanishes exactly when the even part does: \( \int_{-[[h]]}^{[[h]]} \left( ([[c2]])x^{2} + k \right) dx = 2\left(\frac{[[c2]]}{3}[[h]]^{3} + k[[h]]\right) = 0 \), which gives \( k = -\frac{[[c2]]}{3}[[h]]^{2} = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This drops the minus sign when solving \( \tfrac{c_2}{3}h^2 + k = 0 \) for \( k \).
This uses \( h \) instead of \( h^{2} \), as if the \( x^{2} \) term contributed only one power of \( h \).
This forgets to divide by 3 when isolating \( k \).
This wrongly assumes no constant shift is ever needed, ignoring the nonzero even term \( c_2 x^2 \).
This is off by one from the correct value.
This is off by one from the correct value.
This is off by two from the correct value.

stem: Evaluate \( \int_{[[m]]-[[h]]}^{[[m]]+[[h]]} \left( (x-([[m]]))^{3} + (x - ([[m]])) + ([[k]]) \right) dx \), using symmetry about the center \( x=[[m]] \) of the interval.
solution: Substituting \( u = x - [[m]] \) centers the interval at 0: the integral becomes \( \int_{-[[h]]}^{[[h]]} \left( u^{3} + u + ([[k]]) \right) du \). The odd terms \( u^{3} \) and \( u \) vanish by symmetry, leaving only the constant: \( 2([[k]])([[h]]) = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This wrongly keeps the odd terms \( u^3 \) and \( u \) as if they doubled instead of vanishing.
This wrongly keeps the \( u \) term's contribution alongside the correct constant term.
This forgets to double the constant term's contribution.
This mistakenly reintroduces the center \( m \) as if the shift did not remove it.
This is off by 2 from the correct value.
This is off by 2 from the correct value.
This is off by 4 from the correct value.
